evals.prompt: I'm trying to document our payment processing system. Here's what we have: a React frontend where customers check out, a Node.js API that handles order logic, a PostgreSQL database for orders and inventory, and we integrate with Stripe for payments and SendGrid for order confirmation emails. Can you create a C4 container diagram for this? Use Structurizr DSL.
evals.expected_output: A valid Structurizr DSL workspace with a container diagram showing the payment system's internal containers (React frontend, Node.js API, PostgreSQL database) and external systems (Stripe, SendGrid), with specific relationship labels and technology annotations.
expectations: Output contains a valid Structurizr DSL workspace block with model and views sections
The model defines a person representing the customer
The model defines containers for the React frontend, Node.js API, and PostgreSQL database — each with technology specified
Stripe and SendGrid are modeled as external software systems, not containers inside the main system
Relationships have specific intent labels, not just 'Uses' — e.g., 'Submits orders to', 'Stores order data in', 'Processes payments via'
Inter-container relationships include technology/protocol labels like JSON/HTTPS, JDBC, or similar
The views section includes a container view for the payment system
The diagram has a title that includes 'Container' and the system name
The output includes styles or at least mentions how to render the DSL (e.g., structurizr.com/dsl)

evals.prompt: We're designing a new internal tool for our company. It's a support ticket system — support agents will use a web app to manage tickets, customers will submit tickets through our public website and via email. We want to use a message queue to decouple the ingestion from processing. We'll need a database and probably a notification service to alert agents of new high-priority tickets. Help me think through the architecture. Draw C4 diagrams at the right levels to work through this design.
evals.expected_output: C4 diagrams at multiple levels (at minimum system context and container) that help work through the design, with proper notation, element labeling, and specific relationship descriptions.
expectations: A System Context diagram is produced showing the ticket system, its users (support agents, customers), and any external systems
A Container diagram is produced showing the internal architecture (web app, message queue, database, notification service, and any other containers identified)
Each diagram has a title stating its type and scope
People are distinguished by role — support agents and customers are separate, not collapsed into a generic 'User'
Containers have technology suggestions (even if tentative, they should propose concrete choices rather than leaving technology blank)
The message queue is represented as a container with appropriate technology suggestion
Relationship labels describe intent specifically — not 'Uses' but 'Submits tickets via', 'Reads tickets from', 'Publishes new ticket events to', etc.
The diagrams help reason about the design — they're not just transcribing what the user said but also surfacing architectural decisions

evals.prompt: Can you create a Mermaid C4 diagram of our microservice setup? We have an API Gateway (Kong), three backend services: User Service (Go), Order Service (Java/Spring Boot), and Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI). Each service has its own database — User has PostgreSQL, Order has MySQL, Inventory has MongoDB. Services communicate via gRPC. There's also a RabbitMQ message broker for async events between services. The whole thing is used by a mobile app and a web app.
evals.expected_output: A valid Mermaid C4Container diagram with correct syntax, all containers with technology labels, boundaries, and specific relationship labels including protocols.
expectations: Output uses the C4Container Mermaid diagram type
All six containers are present: API Gateway, User Service, Order Service, Inventory Service, and the mobile and web apps (or they appear as persons/external depending on scope interpretation)
Each database is represented with ContainerDb syntax, not plain Container
Each container and database includes its technology in the element declaration
The system boundary groups the internal services together, with the mobile and web apps outside or as separate actors
Service-to-service relationships specify gRPC as the protocol
Service-to-database relationships specify the appropriate protocol (JDBC, driver, etc.)
RabbitMQ is present as a container (queue type) with relationships showing which services publish/consume
Relationship labels are specific about what flows — not generic 'Uses' labels
The diagram includes a title

evals.prompt: I need a quick system context diagram for our analytics platform. It's used by data analysts and product managers. The platform pulls data from our main PostgreSQL production database, from a Salesforce CRM, and from Google Analytics. It also exports reports to a Slack workspace. Can you draw this in ASCII? I want to paste it into a PR description.
evals.expected_output: An ASCII C4 system context diagram showing the analytics platform, its users (data analysts, product managers), and external systems (production database, Salesforce, Google Analytics, Slack), with clear labels and a legend.
expectations: The output is a text-based ASCII diagram, not Mermaid or Structurizr syntax
The analytics platform is shown as the central element
Data analysts and product managers appear as separate people/roles, not collapsed into one
All four external systems are present: production database, Salesforce CRM, Google Analytics, Slack
External systems are visually distinguished from the main system (e.g., dashed borders or explicit 'Ext. System' labels)
Relationship labels describe what flows and in which direction — e.g., 'Pulls sales data from', 'Exports reports to', not just 'Uses'
The diagram has a title stating it's a System Context diagram for the analytics platform
A legend or key is included explaining the notation used
The diagram is reasonably readable as plain text — box alignment doesn't need to be pixel-perfect but the layout should be clear
